coredata.dc:title: Two-photon NIR-responsive carbon dots incorporated into NMOFs for targeted photodynamic therapy

Pathak, Nitesh Kumar

coredata.dc:description: In recent years, Photodynamic therapy (PDT) has shown great potential in clinics to treat cancer. Due to the limited depth range of UV or visible laser light penetration, the eradication of solid tumors using traditional photosensitizer is difficult. Here, we report the development of a new NIR active photosensitizer covalently conjugated to nanoscale metal-organic frameworks (NMOFs) for the eradication of deep-seated tumors. This photosensitizer can generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the presence of near-infrared light irradiation (980 nm) for PDT. Therefore, we use a strategy to develop two-photon absorbing carbon dots with a fluorescence quantum yield of 13%. The NMOFs are fabricated in the presence of folic acid, which enables them to target cancer cells that overexpress the folate receptor. NMOFs are used as a nanocarrier for CDs and are employed to achieve targeted PDT. This study is the first to observe that carbon dots embedded in NMOF composite could generate singlet oxygen (1O2) under the 980 nm laser light irradiation and also specifically target cancer cells. At a concentration of 100 μg/ml, the nanocomposite demonstrated a 73% rate of cell inhibition. The primary results reported here will show great potential in the future to develop multifunctional carbon dots for targeted two-photon PDT in the presence of higher wavelength light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Two-photon NIR-responsive carbon dots incorporated into NMOFs for targeted photodynamic therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, Photodynamic therapy (PDT) has shown great potential in clinics to treat cancer. Due to the limited depth range of UV or visible laser light penetration, the eradication of solid tumors using traditional photosensitizer is difficult. Here, we report the development of a new NIR active photosensitizer covalently conjugated to nanoscale metal-organic frameworks (NMOFs) for the eradication of deep-seated tumors. This photosensitizer can generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the presence of near-infrared light irradiation (980 nm) for PDT. Therefore, we use a strategy to develop two-photon absorbing carbon dots with a fluorescence quantum yield of 13%. The NMOFs are fabricated in the presence of folic acid, which enables them to target cancer cells that overexpress the folate receptor. NMOFs are used as a nanocarrier for CDs and are employed to achieve targeted PDT. This study is the first to observe that carbon dots embedded in NMOF composite could generate singlet oxygen (1O2) under the 980 nm laser light irradiation and also specifically target cancer cells. At a concentration of 100 μg/ml, the nanocomposite demonstrated a 73% rate of cell inhibition. The primary results reported here will show great potential in the future to develop multifunctional carbon dots for targeted two-photon PDT in the presence of higher wavelength light irradiation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) has received a lot of attention in the clinical treatment of several types of malignancies, where photosensitizer, excitation light for therapy, and oxygen molecules play a significant role. The cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS) is generated with the involvement of an irradiation source and photosensitizer. PDT is more effective in early-stage cancers due to the generation of ROS within a very short distance [1–3]. Here, the photosensitizer is activated by either ultraviolet or visible light, where light enters into the tissues only up to a depth of a few millimeters; subsequently, PDT is not considered an effective treatment methodology when it is operated on large and solid tumors. Simultaneously, targeting the selective tumor site by the reported photosensitizer is very low in numbers, which causes a possible toxic effect on normal healthy cells. Thus, it is crucial to develop an efficient system to generate ROS in the NIR region that is capable of deep penetration to tumor tissues as well as delivering the photosensitizers to the target cells.

In the last few years, numerous photosensitizers have been developed for cancer therapy, e.g., organic molecules, chlorin e6, Porphyrin and its derivatives, graphene quantum dots, black phosphorus, semiconducting polymer nanoparticles (NPs), etc. [4-9] Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a kind of promising photosensitizer for realizing effective PDT to treat cancer due to their superior biocompatibility, stable fluorescence emission, and facile surface functionalization [10,11]. For example, Xu et al. prepared carbon dots using methylene blue and phosphate hydrothermally for enhanced PDT in the presence of a 590 nm laser having an intensity of 88 mW/cm2 [12]. Recently, Zhao et al. developed S, N doped carbon dots using polythiophene derivative as a precursor for imaging-guided PDT under white light irradiation. The above CDs possessed lysosome and mitochondria targeting ability and the pH-sensitive property makes it a potential PDT agent [13]. Yue et al. synthesized CDs using a Ruthenium complex for bioimaging and PDT under white light irradiation [14]. Due to the small size (generally less than 10 nm), the effective accumulation of CDs in tumor sites is very poor, which reduces the theranostic outcomes. Simultaneously, the absorption/emission of CDs is mostly located in the visible region, with only a few exceptions. Although a few CDs-based nanocomposites have been reported for PDT, the real issue is poor cell internalization. Research into nanoparticle-cell membrane interactions involves dissecting physicochemical parameters' roles, such as chemical composition and size, with surface charge and hydrophobic effects playing significant roles. Researchers have evaluated the effect of SLBs (supported lipid bilayers) surface charge and topography, particularly membrane defects, on nanoparticle-lipid membrane interactions revealing distinct responses between homogeneous and inhomogeneous membranes [15,16]

More recently, photosensitizers with two-photon absorption properties have attracted a great deal of attention in PDT [17,18]. Two-photon excitation (TPE) is a nonlinear optical process, that comprises the absorption of two lower-energy photons. To develop a two-photon-based PDT, two low-energy photons are used for photosensitizer activation. With the use of the two-photon excitation strategy, many researchers have developed NIR-responsive photosensitizers that provide advantages like (i) significantly reduced photodamage and photobleaching, (ii) higher spatial tissue penetration, (iii) controlled ROS generation at the intensive area. In this context, a few studies have primarily focused on the fabrication of two-photon active carbon dots for PDT. For example, Yi et al. synthesized red emissive two-photon CDs from p-aminoazobenzene for PDT under 638 nm laser irradiation. Combined with PDT, CDs also endow nucleolus targeting ability and real-time monitoring of fluorescence during the PDT process [19]. Similarly, Nasrin et al. developed CDs from folic acid and curcumin hydrothermally for two-photon responsive PDT in the presence of a 780 nm laser source. The above-mentioned CDs have the self-targeting ability of the nucleus and have been used as a potential theranostic agent [20]. Bai et al. reported S, N doped CDs for two-photon excited fluorescence imaging and synergistic therapy [21]. Although numerous carbon dots have been investigated for photodynamic therapy (PDT) utilizing two-photon absorption, the reported maximum wavelength for laser excitation is 840 nm. To the best of our knowledge, the application of CDs in the field of PDT in the presence of a higher wavelength of light (>900 nm) is not reported. Hence, it is highly needed to develop therapeutic CDs with long wavelength excitation and further targeted to cancer cells.

To increase cellular uptake and targeting to cancer cells, we have developed the conjugations of two-photon absorption carbon dots with nanoscale Metal-Organic Frameworks (NMOFs). In recent years, NMOFs have been used to encapsulate different chemotherapeutics due to their enlarged surface areas, ultrahigh porosity, and tunable functionality [22–25]. In our previous works, we have also developed different NMOFs for targeted drug delivery to specific cancer cells [26,27]. Folic acid is a naturally occurring nutrient present in food and often taken as a dietary supplement. The high affinity of folic acid (FA) for folate receptors on cancer cells enables targeted delivery of anticancer agents, exploiting receptor overexpression for precise treatment, sparing healthy cells [28]. However, there is no report about the modification of NMOFs for targeted delivery of carbon dots to enhance the PDT. The conjugation of the targeting ligand on the NMOFs is a potential task of PDT. Therefore, it is a challenge to develop targeted NMOFs in a facile approach to deliver two-photon absorption carbon dots.

In the present study, we established CDs with an absorption redshift effect as a novel two-photon active photosensitizer (PS). The CDs are developed solvothermally using pyromellitic acid as a carbon source, p-phenylenediamine, and 1,2,4-Triaminobenzene dihydrochloride as nitrogen sources. The developed CDs possess a broad absorption band ranging from 460 nm to 570 nm. CDs have good water dispersibility, high photostability & biocompatibility, and show two-photon absorption in the presence of 980 nm NIR light. To obtain high loading capacity which boosts up 1O2 production, and regulated release to prevent burst effect, the created NMOFs were employed as PS nanocarriers. Smaller-sized CDs easily pass through cell membranes and reduce the number of CDs that are taken up by cells. Therefore, we impregnated CDs on MOFs to make them more difficult to escape from cells and to boost therapeutic efficacy. Scheme 1 shows two steps. First, MIL-53 (Fe) was developed with the encapsulation of folic acid as a targeting ligand in a one-pot synthesis method. Second, carbon dots were conjugated on the surface of NMOFs through EDC/NHS chemistry for the first time. Since the NMOFs were able to incorporate a large number of CDs (photosensitizer) on their surface, maximum photosensitizer internalization occurred in cancer cells, which CDs could not accomplish when used alone. Thus, the conjugation of CDs on the NMOF surface resulted in an enhanced PDT effect. More importantly, the obtained NMOFs/CDs composite exhibited a photodynamic effect under 980 nm laser irradiation.

1,2,4-Triaminobenzene dihydrochloride (1,2,4-TABDH), Terephthalic acid (H2BDC, 99%), Pyromellitic acid (PA, 98%), Folic acid (FA, 98%) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP, 98%) were purchased from Tokyo Chemicals Industry Co., Ltd. p-phenylenediamine (p-PD, 97%) was bought from Alfa Aesar. Ferric chloride (FeCl3) and ethanol were obtained from Merck. N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) was acquired from Rankem Avantor Performance Materials, India Ltd. 1,3-diphenyl-isobenzofuran (DPBF), EDC, NHS, and 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was bought from Sigma Aldrich. 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein 3′,6′-diacetate (DCFH-DA), and Formaldehyde were procured from Thermofisher Scientific. Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM), Phosphate buffered saline (PBS), Fetal bovine serum (FBS), Fluoromount G, and Poly-D-Lysine were purchased from Gibco New York. Millipore water was used during the entire experiment. All the chemicals were used without any further purification.

Different instruments used to characterize the synthesized nanocomposites were a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2600, Japan), Perkin Elmer LS 55 fluorescence spectrophotometer, Talos F200X G2 high-resolution transmission electron microscope(HRTEM), Field emission scanning electron microscope (Supra 55, Carl Zeiss, Germany), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Agilent Cary 660 instrument), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Rigaku, Smartlab with Cu Kα, wavelength 1.5406 Å), X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS, PHI, 5000 versa probe III), Micro-Raman spectroscopy (HORIBA Scientific, France, LabRAM HR-UV-Open, Laser excitation wavelength 532 nm), Gas Sorption Analyzer (Micromeritics Instrument Corporation, USA, 3FLEX 3500), TGA-DTA (Perkin Elmer STA6000, under nitrogen atmosphere with a nitrogen flow rate of 50 ml min−1 and a heating rate of 20 °C min−1), Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern, UK), MDL-H-976 nm-3 W-15050266 (CNI optoelectronics Tech. Co., China), Microplate Reader (Thermo Scientific, Multiskan FC), Leica DMi8 epi-fluorescence microscope and Electron Paramagnetic Resonance Spectrometer (JEOL, Japan Model: JES – X320).

A one-pot solvothermal technique was used to make red fluorescent carbon dots. 1 mmol of pyromellitic acid, 0.25 mmol of 1,2,4-Triaminobenzene dihydrochloride, and 1 mmol of p-phenylenediamine were first dispersed in 25 ml of DMF and then kept for a 20-minute sonication process. The resultant solution was then put into a 30 ml Teflon-lined autoclave and heated for 8 h at 200 °C. The reactant solution was centrifuged for 10 minutes at 5000 rpm to remove big particles after allowing it to cool to ambient temperature. After being filtered through a 0.22 µm syringe filter, the supernatant was dialyzed (MWCO 3500 Da) for 24 h against distilled water. The purified CDs were obtained after freeze-drying.

MIL-FA NMOFs were developed by modifying a previously reported approach [29]. Briefly, 3.75 mmol of H2BDC and 2.5 mmol of FeCl3 were dissolved in 43 ml of DMF under stirring for 15 min, and then 2 mg of Folic acid mixed in 2 ml DMF was added dropwise to this solution. The reaction mixture was heated in a round bottom flask up to 70 °C for 8 h under vigorous stirring. The resulting product was further centrifuged, rinsed with ethanol 4 times, and finally dried in a vacuum oven. For comparison study, MIL-53 (Fe) was synthesized similarly without the addition of folic acid.

At first, the COOH group functionalized NMOFs were activated using EDC/NHS chemistry, and then CD solution was added. Briefly, 45 mg EDC and 45 mg NHS were added to the MIL-FA solution, and the solution was left under dark conditions for 3 h to activate the acid groups. After that, 15 ml of CD solution was poured into it, and the reaction was allowed to proceed in the dark for 24 h. The produced MIL-FA@CDs solution was then centrifuged and rinsed with distilled water several times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the quantum yield of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Rhodamine 6 G in distilled water is used as a reference (QY = 95%). The following Eq. (1) was used to calculate the quantum yield of CDs
(1) ɸs = ɸr (Is/Ir) (ηs/ηr)2
where ɸ is the quantum yield, I represent the slope of the curve, and η is the solvent’s refractive index. The subscripts “r” and “s” are used for reference and sample, respectively.

HeLa cells and HEK293T were supplied by NCCS (National Centre for Cell Science), Pune. And cells were cultured in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium and supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% antibiotics (penicillin/streptomycin, 100 μg ml−1) maintained at a temperature of 37 °C under a 5% CO2 environment.

The cytotoxicity of MIL-FA@CDs in HeLa and HEK293T cells was evaluated using the MTT assay. The cells were seeded with a density of 104 cells per well in a 96-well plate for 24 h. Then, nanocomposite MIL-FA@CDs, CDs, MIL-53, and MIL-FA were added with various concentrations of 0, 12.5, 25, 50, and 100 µg/ml. After 12 h of co-incubation, half of the cells with MIL-FA@CDs were irradiated for 10 min with a 980 nm laser at an intensity of 1 W/cm2. The plate was then incubated at 37 °C for another 4 h with 25 µl of MTT solution (5 mg/ml) in PBS added to each well. 100 µl of DMSO was added to dissolve the formazan crystal, after removing the MTT-containing media from each well. The absorbance of formazan dye was measured using a Multiskan FC microplate reader at 450 nm.

A chemical probe 1,3-diphenyl-isobenzofuran (DPBF) was chosen to detect the formation of singlet oxygen species. The CDs dispersed in ethanol (50 µg/ml) and MIL-FA@CDs dissolved in PBS (50 µg/ml) were mixed with DPBF solution (0.5 mM, 100 µl) and then exposed to a 980 nm laser. At an interval of one minute, the DPBF absorption intensity was measured at 412 nm. It was seen that the production of singlet oxygen resulted in a decrease in DPBF absorption intensity. The produced singlet oxygen combines with the pyranoid ring of DPBF, causing ring opening and a decrease in absorbance at 412 nm.

The time-dependent internalization of MIL-FA@CDs was assessed using HeLa cells and HEK293T cells. The cells were seeded on a glass coverslip (treated with poly-D-Lysine) inside a 24-well plate with a density of 30,000 cells per well and incubated for 48 h for attachment to the glass surface. Cells were further treated with 50 µg/ml of nanocomposite MIL-FA@CDs and incubated for 3 h, 6 h, 12 h, and 24 h at 37 °C. After incubation for specific time intervals, the cells were fixed with 4% formaldehyde for 10 minutes at room temperature. Further, the fixed cells were washed with PBS and then the coverslip was mounted on a glass slide using Fluoromount-G with DAPI mounting media. Finally, images of the cells were recorded using a Leica DMi8 epi-fluorescence microscope.

Measurement of singlet oxygen generation was performed using DCFH-DA (non-fluorescent) which reacts with 1O2 to form fluorescent DCF (2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein). HeLa cells were seeded in a 96-well plate with a density of 5000 cells per well and incubated for 48 h. The cells were further treated with 50 µg/ml of MIL-FA@CDs. After incubating for 24 h, 10 µM DCFH-DA in DMEM was added to each well for 10 min. After removing the DCFH-DA-containing media, PBS was used to wash the cells twice. The cells were then exposed to a 980 nm laser and imaged using a Leica fluorescence microscope.

Here, we aim to synthesize red emissive carbon dots for photodynamic therapy, as lower wavelength light exposure has a toxic effect on cells. To obtain effective CDs showing red shift emission, we have used 1,2,4-Triaminobenzene dihydrochloride, p-phenylenediamine, and pyromellitic acid as precursors. The CDs having higher nitrogen content were prepared through a solvothermal route in DMF as shown in Fig. S1 . For the first time, in situ attached FA to MIL-53 was developed via a bottom-up approach ( Fig. 1a). The size of the MIL-FA was tuned by varying the amount of folic acid (Fig. 1b-d). For 0.005 mmol, 0.010 mmol, and 0.015 mmol of folic acid in the reaction solution, we observed a significant change in the size of MIL-FA as 190±10 nm, 1210±10 nm, and 1583.3±10 nm, respectively (Fig. 1e-g). As the amount of folic acid increased in the reaction medium, the growth of nucleation was improved, resulting in the larger particle size of the MIL-FA nanocomposite [30]. For photodynamic therapy, the highest degree of cellular internalization of the nanocomposite is desired; hence, for further studies, we utilized the lowest size MIL-FA containing 0.005 mmol of folic acid. In-situ attachment of FA to MIL-53 and fabrication of MIL-FA@CDs were achieved. Firstly, MIL-FA was synthesized, and CDs were conjugated using EDC/NHS chemistry. The photographic images of CDs, MIL-FA, MIL-53(Fe), and MIL-FA@CDs are shown in Fig. S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and physical properties of CDs, MIL-FA, and MIL-FA@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The structural morphology and particle size of the resulting CDs were analyzed using HRTEM. Fig. 2a shows that the spherical CDs were uniformly dispersed, with particle sizes varying from 2 to 5 nm, having an average diameter of 3.13 nm. The particle size histogram for CDs is shown in Fig. 2b. An evident lattice spacing value of 0.21 nm was seen in the HRTEM image of one carbon dot (Fig. 2a Inset), which corresponds to the (100) lattice plane of graphite. [31,32] Raman spectra in Fig. S3 show two distinct Raman bands at 1347 cm−1 and 1583 cm−1 ascribed to disorders (D band) and sp2 configuration (G band), respectively. [31] The ratio of ID/IG for CDs was 0.84, indicating the presence of more sp2-bonded carbon atoms. After conjugating CDs, the MIL-FA@CDs maintained the hexagonal bipyramidal morphology with an average size of 230±10 nm (Fig. 2c,d). The TEM images of MIL-FA and MIL-FA@CDs are shown in Fig. 2e and f, respectively. The rough surface of MIL-FA@CDs indicates the successful loading of CDs.

The XRD pattern of MIL-53 given in Fig. 3a showing diffraction peaks at 2θ = 9.36, 12.5, 17.6, 18.9, and 22.1° matches well with the crystalline MIL-53 reported in the literature. [33] The broad peak at 26.5° similar to graphene was observed in the XRD spectra of CDs and MIL-FA@CDs (Fig. 3a Inset), confirming the successful conjugation of CDs onto MIL-FA. The elemental and EDX mapping confirmed the presence of different elements in the nanocomposite (Fig. S4). Carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), and iron (Fe) were found in the EDX spectrum, and no peak for any other element was observed (Fig. 3b). This result indicates that the synthesized nanocomposite was pure and free from any impurities. The DLS (Dynamic Light Scattering) analysis in Fig. S5a suggested that the average hydrodynamic diameter of CDs, MIL-FA, and MIL-FA@CDs were 24.3±5, 284.7±5, and 326.4±5 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linear optical properties of CDs, MIL-FA, and MIL-FA@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption spectra of newly synthesized CDs show a broad band ranging from 460 nm to 570 nm due to the n-π* transition (Fig. 3c) [34]. PL emissions of CDs were recorded under different excitation wavelengths from 510 nm to 570 nm. The intensity of the emission increases as the excitation wavelength increases and begins to decrease as we move from 540 nm to 570 nm. The CDs exhibit excitation-independent emission with the highest emission peak at 605 nm when excited at 540 nm. This indicates that the red emission of CDs was caused by the lower energy transition at 540 nm (Fig. 3d). This predominant emission peak at 605 nm can also be seen in the fluorescence spectra of MIL-FA@CDs and no such peak was observed in the PL emission spectra of MIL-FA, which demonstrates the successful loading of carbon dots on to the surface of MIL-FA@CDs (Fig. S5b). UV–vis spectroscopy was used to characterize the in-situ attachment of folic acid in MIL-FA, as indicated in Fig. S5c. MIL-53 shows two characteristic absorption bands at 259 nm and 327 nm. The prominent peaks for folic acid at 280 nm and 366 nm are observed due to π-π* and n-π* transition. [35] The appearance of these two peaks at 280 nm and 366 nm in the UV–vis spectra of MIL-FA verifies the successful conjugation of folic acid in MIL-FA. The fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was calculated using a relative method given in Fig. 3e. The fluorescence quantum yield for CDs was 0.13 relative to Rhodamine 6 G (0.95, H2O). The UV–vis-NIR spectra of CDs and MIL-FA@CDs are given in Fig. S6 showing a broad peak. The synthesized nanocomposites MIL-FA and MIL-FA@CDs possessed good stability in PBS (pH 7.4) which greatly increased the possibility of their use in vitro applications (Fig. S7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nonlinear optical properties of CDs and MIL-FA@CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
As the two-photon photodynamic therapy allows greater tissue penetration and more precise targeting of the treatment area, we have explored the possibility of a two-photon absorption coefficient in MIL-FA@CDs under a 980 nm laser source. To achieve this, we have used the OA (open aperture) Z-scan technique to measure the two-photon absorption coefficient (β). The setup configuration diagram is given in Fig. S8. The β value for CDs was also estimated. Fig. 4 illustrates the OA Z-scan curve for both CDs and MIL-FA@CDs at a concentration of 1 mg/ml. The laser power is varied to see the possible effects of intensity on the β value. We have noticed that the OA Z-scan profile exhibits symmetry with respect to the focus (Z = 0). At the focus, we observed a minimal level of transmission. This minimal transmission at the focus signifies the reverse saturable absorption (RSA) effect, and the RSA effect is governed by the two-photon absorption (TPA) process, which yields a positive sign of β [36]. To calculate β, the normalized transmittance of the OA Z-scan was fitted with Eq. (2). [37,38]

= βI0L/2 represents the on-axis phase shift due to nonlinear absorption, and I0 is the laser intensity at the focus.

The
Δ ψ
0
is obtained as a fitting parameter and further
Δ ψ
0
is used to calculate the β value. While fitting the experimental data by Eq. (2), it was observed that the data fits better with the Z0 value of 2.35 mm, which is slightly different from the theoretically calculated Z0 value (2 mm). This variation is anticipated due to uncertainties in measurements such as beam diameter and beam waist. The β values calculated for both CDs and MIL-FA@CDs as a function of laser power are summarized in Table 1 .

We observed that the magnitude of β is slightly higher for CDs compared to MIL-FA@CDs. Furthermore, the magnitude of β for both CDs and MIL-FA@CDs diminishes as the intensity increases. The decrease of the two-photon absorption coefficient with the increase of laser power is attributed to the sequential two-photon absorption process, where a minor contribution from excited-state absorption is feasible along with strong two-photon absorption, and it is supported by previous studies [39,40]. This effect is apparent because there is significantly less absorption at 980 nm for both CDs and MIL-FA@CDs. However, due to the n-π* transition, substantial absorption is evident around 490 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR study, XPS, and Zeta potential
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR spectroscopy was used to confirm the surface functional groups of the CDs, MIL-FA@CDs, and the successful loading of CDs on a metal-organic framework. Fig. 5a shows the FT-IR spectra of MIL-53, MIL-FA, MIL-FA@CDs, FA, and CDs. OH/NH vibrations are responsible for the peak located between 3100 cm−1 and 3450 cm−1 [41]. Amine/carbonyl group stretching vibration produces a peak at 1622 cm−1, while C-O stretching produces a peak at 1314 cm−1. [42] The spectra of CDs and MIL-FA@CDs show peaks at 1728 cm−1 and 1390 cm−1 that are attributable to the COOH group and C-N bond, respectively. The C-N bond signal at 1390 cm−1 indicates that the carbon dots and MIL-FA were successfully conjugated to produce MIL-FA@CDs. For one-pot synthesized MIL-FA, most of the distinct peaks of folic acid at 3400, 3310, 1690, 1600, 1480, 1330, 1190, 1108, and 825 cm−1 confirm the efficient in situ attachment of folic acid on MIL-53 [27]. Additionally, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed to gain further insights into the structural features. XPS spectra were used to analyze the elemental composition of MIL-53, CDs, MIL-FA, and MIL-FA@CDs. The C1s, N1s, O1s, and Fe2p are represented by the significant peaks at 284.5, 399.5, 530.2, and 709.8 eV, respectively. The well-decorated CDs on MIL-FA are indicated by the prominent N1s peak in the XPS spectra of MIL-FA@CDs. The High-resolution XPS (HR-XPS) spectra for each nanomaterial are given in Fig. S9. The HR-XPS spectrum of C1s indicates the presence of C-C/CC (283.67 eV), C-N/C-O (284.75 eV), and CO (287.10 eV) moieties [43]. N1s XPS spectrum is deconvoluted into pyridinic N (397.37 eV), pyrrolic N (398.95 eV), and graphitic N (400.48 eV). The deconvoluted HR-XPS spectra of O1s illustrate the presence of CO (530.55 eV) and C-OH (532.69 eV). A peak at 536.39 eV was ascribed to the gas phase water peak [44]. MIL-53, MIL-FA, and MIL-FA@CDs have the same deconvolution spectra for C1s and N1s; however, an additional element Fe is present, which is further deconvoluted into Fe 2p3/2 (709.29 eV), and Fe 2p1/2 (723.21 eV) [45]. O1s for MIL-53, MIL-FA, and MIL-FA@CDs consist of Fe-O (528.57 eV), CO (530.22 eV), and C-OH (531.52 eV) groups. When compared to MIL-FA, we saw that MIL-53's C1s, N1s, O1s, and Fe2p peak positions were slightly shifted, which may be explained by FA being attached to MIL-53. Zeta potential was employed to investigate the surface charge of CDs, MIL-53, MIL-FA, and MIL-FA@CDs and the strategy of attachment of CDs on MIL-FA. Zeta potential showed the corresponding values of 26.5 mV, −34 mV, 11.3 mV, and −13.9 mV for MIL-53, CDs, MIL-FA, and MIL-FA@CDs (Fig. 5b), verifying that MIL-FA is successfully decorated with CDs to yield MIL-FA@CDs. The CDs with negative terminal oxygen-containing groups were able to couple nicely with positive charged MIL-FA. Further, time-correlated single-photon counting was done to examine the fluorescence lifetime decay of the final composite MIL-FA@CDs and CDs. CDs and MIL-FA@CDs displayed third-order exponential decay with an average lifespan of 4.62 ns and 4.17 ns, respectively. After incorporating CDs onto MIL-FA, the triplet state lifetime of the final nanocomposite was reduced. This can be attributed to the heavy atom effect due to the presence of Fe atom in MIL-FA@CDs. [46] The presence of a heavy atom, Fe will increase not only the S1→T1 but also enhance T1→S0 relaxation, potentially shortening the triplet state lifetime in the nanocomposite. Fig. S10a,b depicts the triplet state lifetime decay curve of CDs and MIL-FA @CDs. Fig. S10c shows the exponential decay times (τ1, τ2, and τ3) of CDs and MIL-FA@CDs with their area proportions. τ1 is responsible for the electronic transition of the carbon core whereas the electronic transitions in the surface state are attributed to τ2 and τ3. The triple-exponential decay of composites suggests that fluorescence decay is highly dependent on surface/edge states.

Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis was performed to investigate the surface area and porosity. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm for MIL-53, MIL-FA, and MIL-FA@CDs is given in Fig. S11a. MIL-53 displayed a surface area of 62.204 m2/g (BJH method) and a pore volume of 0.1899 cc/g. The surface area of in-situ synthesized MIL-FA was 75.694 m2/g, with a pore volume of 0.3014 cc/g. Surface area and pore volume were both slightly increased in the case of MIL-FA. It is possible that the active involvement of folic acid in the framework formation increased the surface area of MIL-FA and led to an increase in the pore volume. As a result, MIL-FA with the required surface area and large pore volume, was chosen as the metal-organic framework for CD loading. The nanocomposite MIL-FA@CDs exhibited a surface area of 48.668 m2/g and a pore volume of 0.2661 cc/g. Loading of carbon dots on MIL-FA resulted in a decrease in surface area and pore volume, which revealed the successful decoration of CDs on the surface of MIL-FA. As a result, the produced nanocomposite is extremely capable of increased photosensitizer loading for PDT.

The thermal stability and the fabrication of nanocomposite were studied by thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA). The TGA curves of MIL-53, MIL-FA, CDs, and MIL-FA@CDs are given in Fig. S11b. The initial weight loss at 100 °C was noticed due to the elimination of adsorbed water molecules from the pores of MIL-53 and MIL-FA. Next, decay was seen at 200 °C, corresponding to the disintegration of chemical bonds associated with NMOFs. The subsequent weight loss in the range between 200 and 500 °C shows the decomposition of functional groups containing oxygen. The final weight loss at 600 °C was attributed to the complete decomposition of MOFs. The TGA curve for MIL-53 (Fe) was found to be similar to MIL-FA, having thermal stability up to 480 °C with 70% weight loss, and the TGA graph for CDs and MIL-FA@CDs was alike, having thermal stability up to 600 °C and a total weight loss of 95%. This confirmed that the CDs were attached fruitfully on MIL-FA.

To detect the singlet oxygen generation ability of CDs and MIL-FA@CDs, DPBF was used as a chemical probe. The absorption changes of DPBF ( Fig. 6a), CDs (Fig. 6b), MIL-FA@CDs (Fig. 6c), CDs+DPBF (Fig. 6d), and MIL-FA@CDs+DPBF (Fig. 6e) under 980 laser irradiation were recorded from 0 to 12 min. The absorption spectra of CDs, DPBF, and MIL-FA@CDs showed no significant change, whereas the absorption spectra of CDs+DPBF and MIL-FA@CDs+DPBF were seen decreasing at 412 nm as the irradiation time increased. The absorption peak of DPBF decreased as it was consumed by the produced 1O2, indicating the efficacy of MIL-FA@CDs to be used as an effective PDT agent under the irradiation of NIR light. Electron Paramagnetic Resonance Spectroscopy further confirmed the 1O2 generation ability of CDs and MIL-FA@CDs under 980 nm laser irradiation (Fig. S16). 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) is used as a 1O2 trapper. Fig. S16 displayed distinct signals associated with 1O2 -induced TEMP-1-oxyl in the ESR spectra when subjected to laser irradiation for 2 min. The comparative table of reported carbon dots for photodynamic therapy is given in Table S1.

When a photosensitizer molecule is exposed to laser light, the ground state electrons of the photosensitizer are excited to a higher energy level called an excited singlet state. The excited singlet state undergoes intersystem crossing (ISC), producing a stable excited triplet state. The stable triplet state provides enough time for the photosensitizer to collide with oxygen molecules, therefore undergoing energy transfer and resulting in the formation of singlet oxygen species. Here, we used newly synthesized carbon dots as photosensitizers and a 980 nm laser as a light source. We used a 980 nm laser source to irradiate the nanocomposites to evaluate the effectiveness of two-photon PDT. The steady reduction in the absorbance value of DPBF at 412 nm with increasing irradiation time, confirms the generation of 1O2 as shown in Fig. 6d and e.

The cytotoxicity of MIL-FA@CDs in HeLa cells with and without laser irradiation was evaluated using the MTT assay. Cells were examined after being treated with varied concentrations (0, 12.5, 25, 50, 100 μg/ml) of CDs, MIL-53, MIL-FA, and MIL-FA@CDs. The nanomaterials synthesized showed no significant cytotoxicity in the 0–100 μg/ml range, indicating good biocompatibility. However, after 10 minutes of laser irradiation, the HeLa cells incubated with MIL-FA@CDs showed obvious inhibition, as shown in Fig. 6 f. When exposed to NIR light, the synthesized nanocomposites demonstrated concentration-dependent cell death. After 24 h incubation, only 30% of cancer cells were alive when the CDs concentration was 100 μg/ml. Whereas the cell viability dropped to 27% for 100 μg/ml of MIL-FA@CDs. This slight decrease in cell viability suggested that the targeting ability of MIL-FA@CDs resulted in increased cellular uptake and greater 1O2 generation. Additionally, an MTT assay in HEK293T was also recorded and the results showed that at the maximum concentration, the cell viability was >80% ( Fig. S12 ). All these results confirmed the ability of MIL-FA@CDs to efficiently produce singlet oxygen. Hence, it can be used as an excellent PDT agent.

In vitro cellular uptake study of MIL-FA@CDs was performed in the HeLa cell line, and the fluorescence images were taken using 535 nm excitation as shown in Fig. 7 . The cells were incubated for 3 h, 6 h, 12 h, and 24 h. DAPI (blue fluorescence) was used for nuclei staining. The images show that the MIL-FA@CDs (orange fluorescence) are mainly localized in the cytoplasm. We observed that the nanocomposite MIL-FA@CDs was mostly internalized within 3 h. A comparative study of cellular internalization of MIL-FA@CDs for the HeLa cell line and HEK293T cell line shown in Fig. S13 was also conducted for a 3 h incubation period. We noticed that significant internalization did not occur for the nanocomposite MIL-FA@CDs in the HEK293T cell line probably due to lack of high concentration of folate receptors on the cell surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro singlet oxygen generation study of MIL-FA@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Using the ROS indicator DCFH-DA, the ability to produce singlet oxygen was evaluated in four groups: (i) DCFH-DA, (ii) DCFH-DA + hυ, (iii) MIL-FA@CDs + DCFH-DA, (iv) CDs + DCFH-DA + hυ, and (v) MIL-FA@CDs + DCFH-DA + hυ. Groups (i) and (iii) of non-irradiated cells showed no fluorescence in the green channel, as shown in Fig. 8 (i and iii). This demonstrated that in the absence of laser irradiation MIL-FA@CDs and DCFH-DA were unable to form 1O2 showing negligible toxicity. Group (ii) in Fig. 8 (ii) showed no fluorescence after the cells were exposed to a 980 nm laser for 2 minutes, but group (iv) and (v) in Fig. 8 (iv), (v) showed a green fluorescence due to the formation of DCF confirming that DCFH-DA in presence of light does not produce 1O2 whereas CDs and MIL-FA@CDs were able to produce 1O2 effectively under light irradiation. Quantitative investigation (Fig. S14) shows that cells treated with MIL-FA@CDs have 1.40 times higher DCF fluorescence intensity than cells incubated with CDs. This difference in DCF fluorescence intensity can be explained by the targeting action of the nanocomposite MIL-FA@CDs, which leads to increased internalization CDs compared to CDs alone. At the same time, the contracting of cells to a spherical shape was also observed confirming the killing of HeLa cells by 1O2 generation. In addition, we irradiated the cells without MIL-FA@CDs and noticed that the cell morphology did not change on laser exposure for 2 min Fig. S15. All these results proved that the MIL-FA@CDs have the least toxicity in dark conditions and exhibit toxicity when exposed to laser irradiation due to the production of 1O2.

In summary, two-photon NIR active CDs were effectively produced as a photosensitizer embedded on an in situ prepared folic acid attached metal-organic framework, termed as MIL-FA@CDs for NIR responsive targeted PDT. The largest number of CDs were able to conjugate onto the surface of MOF due to its highly porous structure. The nanocomposite MIL-FA@CDs demonstrated excellent biocompatibility, good water dispersibility, and stable PL properties. MIL-FA@CDs interacted with the cancer cells via the folate receptor and were mostly localized in the cytoplasm of the cell. Higher spatial tissue penetration and the targeting ability of MIL-FA@CDs amplified the therapeutic effect of PDT, resolving the limitations of PDT. A desirable in vitro cell killing was accomplished under a 980 nm light source. Thus, MIL-FA@CDs exhibited a high yield of 1O2, demonstrating their superiority as a PDT agent. Additionally, this nanocomposite can be further used in various cancer cell lines for targeted theranostics approaches. Future research could involve developing nanocomposites for combinational therapies to enhance photodynamic efficacy and overcome tumor hypoxia.
